732	
733	## src/astrolabe.ts Shape
734	
735	The main apparatus factory. Follows the `createClerk()` / `createSpider()` factory pattern:
736	
737	```typescript
738	export function createAstrolabe(): Plugin {
739	  let plansBook: Book<PlanDoc>;
740	
741	  const api: AstrolabeApi = { ... };
742	
743	  // Three clockwork engine designs (imported from engines/)
744	  // rig template + mapping (inline or imported)
745	
746	  return {
747	    apparatus: {
748	      requires: ['stacks', 'clerk', 'spider', 'loom', 'fabricator'],
749	      consumes: [],          // astrolabe doesn't read kit contributions
750	      provides: api,
751	      supportKit: {
752	        books: {
753	          plans: {
754	            indexes: ['status', 'codex', 'createdAt'],
755	          },
756	        },
757	        engines: {
758	          'astrolabe.plan-init': planInitEngine,
759	          'astrolabe.inventory-check': inventoryCheckEngine,
760	          'astrolabe.decision-review': decisionReviewEngine,
761	        },
762	        writTypes: [
763	          { name: 'brief', description: 'A patron brief triggering the planning pipeline' },
764	        ],
765	        roles: {
766	          sage: {
767	            permissions: [
768	              'astrolabe:read',
769	              'astrolabe:write',
770	              'clerk:read',
771	              'clerk:write',
772	              'stacks:read',
773	            ],
774	          },
775	        },
776	        rigTemplates: {
777	          planning: planningTemplate,
778	        },
779	        rigTemplateMappings: {
780	          brief: 'astrolabe.planning',  // qualified name
781	        },
782	        tools: [
783	          planShowTool,
784	          planListTool,
785	          inventoryWriteTool,
786	          scopeWriteTool,
787	          decisionsWriteTool,
788	          observationsWriteTool,
789	          specWriteTool,
790	        ],
791	      },
792	      start(ctx: StartupContext): void {
793	        const stacks = guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks');
794	        plansBook = stacks.book<PlanDoc>('astrolabe', 'plans');
795	      },
796	    },
797	  };
798	}
799	```
800	
801	---
802	
803	## src/types.ts Shape
804	
805	```typescript
806	export interface PlanDoc {
807	  [key: string]: unknown;
808	  id: string;
809	  codex: string;
810	  status: 'reading' | 'analyzing' | 'reviewing' | 'writing' | 'completed' | 'failed';
811	  inventory?: string;
812	  observations?: string;
813	  scope?: ScopeItem[];
814	  decisions?: Decision[];
815	  spec?: string;
816	  generatedWritId?: string;
817	  createdAt: string;
818	  updatedAt: string;
819	}
820	
821	export interface ScopeItem { ... }
822	export interface Decision { ... }
823	
824	export interface AstrolabeConfig {
825	  generatedWritType?: string;
826	}
827	
828	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
829	  interface GuildConfig {
830	    astrolabe?: AstrolabeConfig;
831	  }
832	}
833	
834	// AstrolabeApi: runtime API via guild().apparatus<AstrolabeApi>('astrolabe')
835	export interface AstrolabeApi {
836	  // (probably empty or minimal — tools access the book directly)
837	}
838	```
839	
840	---
841	
842	## Engines Location and Import
843	
844	Engines are clockwork (implement `EngineDesign` from `@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus`). They go in `src/engines/`:
845	- `src/engines/plan-init.ts` — exports `planInitEngine: EngineDesign`
846	- `src/engines/inventory-check.ts` — exports `inventoryCheckEngine: EngineDesign`
847	- `src/engines/decision-review.ts` — exports `decisionReviewEngine: EngineDesign`
848	- `src/engines/index.ts` — re-exports all three
849	
850	The engines use `guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks')` at runtime (not at module load time). They also use `generateId` from `@shardworks/nexus-core` (for `decision-review`'s InputRequestDoc id).
851	
852	---
853	
854	## Critical: Engine ID Namespacing
855	
856	Engine IDs in the Fabricator registry must be globally unique. The Fabricator uses the engine `id` field as the key:
857	
858	```typescript
859	// fabricator.ts — EngineRegistry:
860	private registerFromKit(kit: Record<string, unknown>, pluginId: string): void {
861	  const rawEngines = kit.engines;
862	  for (const value of Object.values(rawEngines)) {
863	    if (isEngineDesign(value)) {
864	      this.designs.set(value.id, value);
865	```
866	
867	So the engine's `id` field value is what gets registered — NOT the key in the `engines` object. We need:
868	- `planInitEngine.id = 'astrolabe.plan-init'`
869	- `inventoryCheckEngine.id = 'astrolabe.inventory-check'`
870	- `decisionReviewEngine.id = 'astrolabe.decision-review'`
871	
872	And in `supportKit.engines`:
873	```typescript
874	engines: {
875	  'plan-init': planInitEngine,       // key is just for organization; value.id matters
876	  'inventory-check': inventoryCheckEngine,
877	  'decision-review': decisionReviewEngine,
878	},
879	```
880	
881	The Fabricator ignores the key; it uses `value.id`. The Spider's `buildDesignSourceMap` does:
882	```typescript
883	for (const entry of engineEntries) {
884	  this.registerEnginesFromKit(entry.pluginId, { engines: entry.value });
885	}
886	// registerEnginesFromKit: iterates Object.values(kit.engines) and uses value.id as the key
887	```
888	
889	---
890	
891	## AstrolabeApi
892	
893	The Astrolabe doesn't necessarily need to expose a public API (tools access Stacks directly). But the `provides` field should exist for consistency. It could expose:
894	- Nothing (empty object) — tools work directly via stacks
895	- Or minimal helpers used by engines
896	
897	Looking at how engines access the plans book: they need `guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks').book<PlanDoc>('astrolabe', 'plans')`. This is fine — no AstrolabeApi needed by engines.
898	
899	The `provides: api` pattern is still used (even if the api object is empty) for consistency and future extension.

900	
901	---
902	
903	## Existing Tests for Adjacent Code (patterns to follow)
904	
905	- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.test.ts` — uses `setGuild`, `createStacksApparatus`, `MemoryBackend`, builds fake `Guild` object
906	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/input-request.test.ts` — same pattern, tests clockwork engines
907	- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts` — comprehensive rig execution tests
908	
909	Test pattern:
910	```typescript
911	import { describe, it, beforeEach, afterEach } from 'node:test';
912	import assert from 'node:assert/strict';
913	import { setGuild, clearGuild, generateId } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
914	import { createStacksApparatus } from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus';
915	import { MemoryBackend } from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus/testing';
916	```
917	
918	---
919	
920	## Doc/Code Discrepancies
921	
922	1. **Architecture spec says `requires: [clerk, stacks, spider, loom, fabricator]`** (doc). The `loom` apparatus is not called in `start()` — only its kit consumption mechanism is used. Technically, loom could be `recommends`. However, the spec is authoritative for implementation purposes.
923	
924	2. **`rigTemplateMappings` value must be qualified name.** The architecture spec says `brief → template mapping` but doesn't specify whether the mapping value should be the unqualified (`planning`) or qualified (`astrolabe.planning`) name. Code analysis of `RigTemplateRegistry.registerKitMappings` reveals templates are stored under qualified names, so mappings must also use qualified names. The value `'astrolabe.planning'` is correct.
925	
926	3. **Prompt content for anima-session engines.** The architecture doc describes the rig template with `prompt` values for reader/analyst/spec-writer, but the actual prompt text content (what instructions the anima receives) is not specified — that's the `charter.md`/role instructions concern, and/or will be spelled out in Part 2. For MVP Part 1, the template must have non-empty `prompt` strings to pass `anima-session` engine validation.
927	
928	4. **`planId` vs `writ.id`.** The spec says `planId = brief writ ID`. The `plan-init` engine receives the `writ` given (a `WritDoc`) and uses `writ.id` as the plan key. Downstream engines receive `planId` via `$yields.plan-init.planId`. This is consistent throughout.
929	
930	5. **`plan-init` given: `writ` vs `writId`.** The `draft` engine uses `writ.codex` to know which codex to open. The `plan-init` engine also needs `writ.id` and `writ.codex`. Looking at how `draft` engine is configured: `givens: { writ: '$writ' }`. The `plan-init` engine should also receive `writ: '$writ'` so it can use `writ.codex` when creating the PlanDoc.
931	
932	---
933	
934	## Summary of Key Implementation Decisions (Pre-Analysis)
935	
936	These are observations that will become decisions in the analyst pass:
937	
938	1. **npm package name:** `@shardworks/astrolabe` (no `-apparatus` suffix, per spec)
939	2. **Apparatus provides API:** Empty or minimal — tools use Stacks directly. Apparatus still has `provides` field for consistency.
940	3. **Engine IDs:** Must be `astrolabe.plan-init`, `astrolabe.inventory-check`, `astrolabe.decision-review` (namespaced to avoid global conflicts in Fabricator registry).
941	4. **rigTemplateMappings value:** Must be the qualified name `astrolabe.planning` (not unqualified `planning`).
942	5. **Roles permissions:** What permissions does `astrolabe.sage` need? Minimally: `astrolabe:read`, `astrolabe:write`, `clerk:write` (to post the generated writ), `spider:write` (for input-request-answer? — no, that's patron-facing). The role permission analysis depends on what tools the anima needs during planning. `clerk:write` is needed so the spec-writer can post the generated mandate. `clerk:read` for writ-show.
943	6. **Prompt content:** For MVP, minimal static prompts in the template. Full prompt content (mode-specific instructions) is outside the scope of Part 1 per the brief ("8-step pipeline").
944	7. **AstrolabeApi interface:** Minimal/empty since tools go through Stacks directly.
945	8. **`plan-list` filter by `codex`:** Uses a where clause `['codex', '=', codex]`. The `codex` index is declared, making this efficient.
946

file.content:
---

## Existing Tests for Adjacent Code (patterns to follow)

- `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.test.ts` — uses `setGuild`, `createStacksApparatus`, `MemoryBackend`, builds fake `Guild` object
- `packages/plugins/spider/src/input-request.test.ts` — same pattern, tests clockwork engines
- `packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts` — comprehensive rig execution tests

Test pattern:
```typescript
import { describe, it, beforeEach, afterEach } from 'node:test';
import assert from 'node:assert/strict';
import { setGuild, clearGuild, generateId } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
import { createStacksApparatus } from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus';
import { MemoryBackend } from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus/testing';
```

---

## Doc/Code Discrepancies

1. **Architecture spec says `requires: [clerk, stacks, spider, loom, fabricator]`** (doc). The `loom` apparatus is not called in `start()` — only its kit consumption mechanism is used. Technically, loom could be `recommends`. However, the spec is authoritative for implementation purposes.

2. **`rigTemplateMappings` value must be qualified name.** The architecture spec says `brief → template mapping` but doesn't specify whether the mapping value should be the unqualified (`planning`) or qualified (`astrolabe.planning`) name. Code analysis of `RigTemplateRegistry.registerKitMappings` reveals templates are stored under qualified names, so mappings must also use qualified names. The value `'astrolabe.planning'` is correct.

3. **Prompt content for anima-session engines.** The architecture doc describes the rig template with `prompt` values for reader/analyst/spec-writer, but the actual prompt text content (what instructions the anima receives) is not specified — that's the `charter.md`/role instructions concern, and/or will be spelled out in Part 2. For MVP Part 1, the template must have non-empty `prompt` strings to pass `anima-session` engine validation.

4. **`planId` vs `writ.id`.** The spec says `planId = brief writ ID`. The `plan-init` engine receives the `writ` given (a `WritDoc`) and uses `writ.id` as the plan key. Downstream engines receive `planId` via `$yields.plan-init.planId`. This is consistent throughout.

5. **`plan-init` given: `writ` vs `writId`.** The `draft` engine uses `writ.codex` to know which codex to open. The `plan-init` engine also needs `writ.id` and `writ.codex`. Looking at how `draft` engine is configured: `givens: { writ: '$writ' }`. The `plan-init` engine should also receive `writ: '$writ'` so it can use `writ.codex` when creating the PlanDoc.

---

## Summary of Key Implementation Decisions (Pre-Analysis)

These are observations that will become decisions in the analyst pass:

1. **npm package name:** `@shardworks/astrolabe` (no `-apparatus` suffix, per spec)
2. **Apparatus provides API:** Empty or minimal — tools use Stacks directly. Apparatus still has `provides` field for consistency.
3. **Engine IDs:** Must be `astrolabe.plan-init`, `astrolabe.inventory-check`, `astrolabe.decision-review` (namespaced to avoid global conflicts in Fabricator registry).
4. **rigTemplateMappings value:** Must be the qualified name `astrolabe.planning` (not unqualified `planning`).
5. **Roles permissions:** What permissions does `astrolabe.sage` need? Minimally: `astrolabe:read`, `astrolabe:write`, `clerk:write` (to post the generated writ), `spider:write` (for input-request-answer? — no, that's patron-facing). The role permission analysis depends on what tools the anima needs during planning. `clerk:write` is needed so the spec-writer can post the generated mandate. `clerk:read` for writ-show.
6. **Prompt content:** For MVP, minimal static prompts in the template. Full prompt content (mode-specific instructions) is outside the scope of Part 1 per the brief ("8-step pipeline").
7. **AstrolabeApi interface:** Minimal/empty since tools go through Stacks directly.
8. **`plan-list` filter by `codex`:** Uses a where clause `['codex', '=', codex]`. The `codex` index is declared, making this efficient.

content.thinking:
Good, I've read the full inventory. Now let me also verify a few things from the architecture spec and actual code that will inform specific decisions.

Let me now check some specific details:
1. The `anima-session` engine doesn't pass through extra givens (like `planId`) to the session — it only uses `role`, `prompt`, `cwd`, `conversationId`, and `writ`. So `planId` in the givens won't be passed to the anima. The anima would need to receive it in the `prompt` text itself.
2. The `writ` given IS passed through to the session via `metadata`.


3. I'm double-checking the anima-session engine code to confirm these constraints.

content.text: Let me verify a critical detail about how `anima-session` handles extra givens:

content.thinking:
Critical finding: `anima-session` engine only uses `role`, `prompt`, `cwd`, `conversationId`, and `writ` from givens. It does NOT pass through any extra givens like `planId` to the anima. The `planId` must be embedded in the `prompt` text itself so the anima knows which plan to operate on.

This means the template's prompt givens need to contain the planId value. But `prompt` is a single string value — it can't contain inline yield references (Spider only resolves whole-value yield refs). So how does the planId get into the prompt?
 
I'm working through how the anima actually receives the plan context. The writ ID itself could serve as the plan identifier since it's passed to `anima-session`, and the anima might be able to extract or infer the plan from that, but I need to think through whether the anima actually has access to the writ ID in its execution context. I'm weighing a few approaches: I could have an upstream engine compose the full prompt with the planId baked in, or instruct the anima to derive the planId from the writ ID itself, or have it query `plan-list` to find the active plan—though that gets tricky if multiple plans exist. Another option is to have the plan initialization engine pass the planId through a different channel.

Actually, looking back at the architecture docs, I see the pattern now: the system uses inline string interpolation like `${yields.plan-init.planId}` to wire values directly into prompts. The spec-writer example shows how decision summaries get injected the same way, and the prerequisites mention that inline string interpolation needs to be available in the Spider first.

Checking the prerequisites against what's already landed: the anima-session engine is there, QuestionSpec.details exists in the types, but inline string interpolation support isn't implemented yet. That's the blocker—the Spider can only handle whole-value yield references right now, not the embedded interpolation syntax the Astrolabe design requires.

For the MVP, the cleanest approach is having the plan-init engine yield a fully composed prompt string rather than just the planId, so the template can reference it directly as a single yield value. also construct prompt strings. But that feels wrong — plan-init shouldn't know about prompts.

A better option: **the prompts are just static instructions that tell the anima to use `plan-list` to find the active plan for the current codex.** Since the brief's codex is known, and there should only be one active plan for that codex, the anima can find it.

But actually, re-reading the template more carefully — the `writ` is passed as a given to `anima-session`, and `anima-session` passes `metadata: { writId: writ.id }` and sets `GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL: writ.id@nexus.local`. But the anima still doesn't know the writ ID.

Wait — the prompt IS sent to the AI as the initial user message. Could the prompt itself say: "You are working on plan {planId}. Call plan-show to see the current state." But we can't interpolate planId into the prompt string...

The most practical MVP approach: **the prompt instructs the anima to use `plan-list` with status filter to find the current plan**, OR **use `writ-list` to find the active brief, then use the writ ID as the plan ID**. This is a workable MVP approach that doesn't depend on inline interpolation.

Alternatively: the `plan-init` engine could yield the prompt text itself (composing it with the planId baked in). Then the template's prompt given would be `$yields.plan-init.readerPrompt`, `$yields.plan-init.analystPrompt`, `$yields.plan-init.writerPrompt`.

This is actually cleaner. The `plan-init` engine:
- Creates the plan
- Composes the 3 prompt strings with the planId baked in
- Yields `{ planId, readerPrompt, analystPrompt, writerPrompt }`

Then the template references those yielded prompts directly. But now I'm hitting another constraint: the spec-writer prompt also needs the `decisionSummary` from the `decision-review` engine, and without inline interpolation, I can't inject that into the prompt string. The spec-writer would need to either call `plan-show` to fetch the reconciled decisions, or the decisionSummary would need to be passed as a separate given rather than baked into the prompt.

For the MVP, the practical path forward is having each prompt instruct the anima to call `plan-show` to read the full plan state including decisions, rather than trying to pass everything through the initial prompt. This is a real design choice that belongs in the decision register. I could make it even simpler by having the prompt say "find and read the active plan," but that's fragile if multiple plans are active at once.

Actually, thinking about this more carefully—the architecture doc's approach needs inline string interpolation which isn't available yet. The cleanest workaround for MVP Part 1 is having `plan-init` compose the prompt strings directly as yields, baking in the planId right there in the engine. This works with Spider's current capabilities and keeps everything self-contained without requiring changes to the platform.

I think I've got a solid handle on all the decisions now. Time to write out the scope and decision log.

file.content:
# The Astrolabe — API Contract

Status: **Draft**

Package: `@shardworks/astrolabe` · Plugin id: `astrolabe`

> **⚠️ Future state.** The Astrolabe is not yet implemented. This document captures the design as a target for implementation commissions. Several prerequisites must land in the Spider first: the `anima-session` built-in engine, givens inline string interpolation, and the `QuestionSpec.details` field.

---

## Purpose

The Astrolabe refines minimal briefs from the patron into detailed work specifications. When the patron commissions work — often as little as a sentence or two — the Astrolabe takes that raw intent and produces a structured spec: an inventory of the relevant codebase, analytical observations, scoped decisions, and concrete requirements. The final output is a `mandate` writ posted to the Clerk — ready for the Spider to build an implementation rig.

The Astrolabe does **not** execute implementation work (that's the implementation rig's domain). It does **not** modify the original commission writ. It maintains its own books for planning artifacts (inventory, observations, decisions, specs) and provides tools for the animas that staff its engines to read and write those artifacts. The planning flow terminates by posting a new mandate writ — the Astrolabe's output *is* a commission.

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: [clerk, stacks, spider, loom, fabricator]
```

- **Clerk** — the spec-writer engine posts the generated writ as the final output of the planning pipeline.
- **Stacks** — the Astrolabe's own books store planning artifacts: inventory, observations, scope, decisions, and generated specs.
- **Spider** — the Astrolabe contributes a planning rig template and the `brief` → template mapping. Planning engines use the Spider's `patron-input` block type for decision review. The Astrolabe's quick engines use the Spider's built-in `anima-session` engine design.
- **Loom** — the Astrolabe contributes a single planning role shared across all quick engines in the pipeline.
- **Fabricator** — the Astrolabe contributes clockwork engine designs for validation checkpoints.

content.thinking:
The architecture doc explicitly says "givens inline string interpolation" is a prerequisite that hasn't landed yet. The `anima-session` engine exists, and `QuestionSpec.details` exists. So the missing prerequisite is the inline string interpolation feature.

This is a key decision point: how to handle the fact that one of the spec's prerequisites (inline string interpolation) is not yet implemented. For MVP Part 1, the rig template needs to work without it.

My recommended approach: the `plan-init` engine yields composed prompt strings (with planId baked in), so the `anima-session` engine's `prompt` given can be a whole-value yield reference like `$yields.plan-init.readerPrompt`.


Now I'm documenting the scope and key decisions for this approach.

    description: "Book declaration — astrolabe/plans book schema with indexes, registered via supportKit.books"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'Book: astrolabe/plans with full PlanDoc/ScopeItem/Decision schema + indexes'"
    included: true

  - id: S4
    description: "7 tools — plan-show, plan-list, inventory-write, scope-write, decisions-write, observations-write, spec-write"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'All 7 tools'"
    included: true

  - id: S5
    description: "Kit contribution: brief writ type registered with the Clerk via supportKit.writTypes"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'brief writ type → Clerk'"
    included: true

  - id: S6
    description: "Kit contribution: astrolabe.sage role registered with the Loom via supportKit.roles"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'astrolabe.sage role → Loom'"
    included: true

  - id: S7
    description: "3 clockwork engine designs — plan-init, inventory-check, decision-review — registered with the Fabricator via supportKit.engines"
    rationale: "Implied by 'rig template definition (8-step pipeline)' — the template references these engines; they must exist for the template to validate"
    included: true

  - id: S8
    description: "Kit contribution: 8-step rig template + brief → template mapping registered with the Spider via supportKit.rigTemplates and rigTemplateMappings"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'rig template definition (8-step pipeline) + brief → template mapping'"
    included: true

  - id: S9
    description: "Configuration — generatedWritType config field with GuildConfig augmentation"
    rationale: "Explicit in brief: 'Configuration (generatedWritType)'"
    included: true

  - id: S10
    description: "Tests — unit tests for tools, engines, and apparatus wiring using the codebase's in-memory Stacks test pattern"
    rationale: "Implied by the brief — every plugin in the codebase has corresponding tests; this is a standing obligation"
    included: true

input.content:
decisions:
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S1 — Package scaffolding
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D1
    scope: [S1]
    question: "What is the npm package name?"
    options:
      a: "@shardworks/astrolabe — per architecture spec (no -apparatus suffix)"
      b: "@shardworks/astrolabe-apparatus — matching existing apparatus packages in the repo"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec explicitly says 'Package: @shardworks/astrolabe · Plugin id: astrolabe'. Every other apparatus in the repo uses the -apparatus suffix, but the spec is the authoritative source."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "The architecture spec is explicit; follow it."
      stakes: high

  - id: D2
    scope: [S1]
    question: "What dependencies go in the apparatus requires array?"
    options:
      a: "['stacks', 'clerk', 'spider', 'loom', 'fabricator'] — per architecture spec"
      b: "['stacks', 'clerk'] — minimum needed at start(); others as recommends"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec explicitly lists all five. While loom and fabricator aren't called in start(), the spec is authoritative. Kit contribution consumption doesn't require requires (Wire phase handles it), but the spec captures a design intent about what must be installed."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec is explicit and reflects a considered design intent."
      stakes: low

  - id: D3
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should @shardworks/loom-apparatus be added as an npm dependency for type-safe role definitions?"
    options:
      a: "No — use plain object for roles contribution; no loom import"
      b: "Yes — import KitRoleDefinition for type safety via satisfies"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The supportKit roles field is an open record — no type import is required. Adding loom as an npm dep couples the packages. Other kits in the repo contribute roles without importing loom types. However, type safety is valuable."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Follows the pattern of loose coupling via open records; avoids unnecessary cross-package npm dependency."
      stakes: low

  - id: D4
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should the apparatus declare a consumes field?"
    options:
      a: "Omit entirely — Astrolabe doesn't consume kit contributions from others"
      b: "Declare consumes: [] explicitly for clarity"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Clerk omits consumes; Spider declares it because it actually consumes. Omitting is the codebase convention for apparatuses that don't consume."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Codebase convention — only declare consumes when actually consuming."
      stakes: low

  - id: D5
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should recommends include 'oculus' for dashboard support?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — recommends: ['oculus'], matching Clerk and Spider patterns"
      b: "No — omit recommends entirely for MVP"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "Clerk and Spider both recommend oculus because they contribute pages. The Astrolabe MVP doesn't contribute pages. Adding oculus as recommends would be forward-looking but premature."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "No pages contributed; recommends would be empty promise."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S2 — Types
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D6
    scope: [S2]
    question: "What is the exact shape of the PlanDoc type?"
    options:
      a: "Verbatim from architecture spec — all fields as documented"
      b: "Modify to add additional fields for MVP convenience"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec defines the PlanDoc precisely. The types are a contract for other plugins to import."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "The architecture spec defines the canonical type; implement verbatim."
      stakes: high

  - id: D7
    scope: [S2]
    question: "Should PlanDoc status include a 'reviewing' status for the decision-review phase?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — status union is 'reading' | 'analyzing' | 'reviewing' | 'writing' | 'completed' | 'failed' (as in spec)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec includes all six statuses. The decision-review engine transitions to 'reviewing' before blocking, and to 'writing' after patron answers are reconciled."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Spec defines the full status machine; no decision needed."
      stakes: high

  - id: D8
    scope: [S2]
    question: "What should AstrolabeApi expose?"
    options:
      a: "Empty object — tools access Stacks directly, no inter-plugin API needed for MVP"
      b: "Expose show/list/patch methods mirroring ClerkApi pattern"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The Clerk exposes a full API because other plugins (Spider, engines) call it programmatically. The Astrolabe's tools and engines all access Stacks directly — no external caller needs an AstrolabeApi in MVP. Adding methods now would duplicate what tools already do. The provides field still exists (empty object) for future extension."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "No external caller needs the API; engines use Stacks directly. provides field exists as empty object for future extension."
      stakes: low

  - id: D9
    scope: [S2]
    question: "Where should the PlanStatus type alias be defined?"
    options:
      a: "Inline union on the PlanDoc.status field"
      b: "Separate exported type alias PlanStatus"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The status values are referenced by plan-list filter params (z.enum needs the values), by engines for transitions, and potentially by external consumers. A named type makes this reusable."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Reusable across tools (plan-list filter) and engines (status transitions)."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S3 — Book declaration
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D10
    scope: [S3]
    question: "What indexes should the plans book declare?"
    options:
      a: "['status', 'codex', 'createdAt'] — per architecture spec"
      b: "Add compound indexes like ['status', 'codex'] for common queries"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec declares exactly these three indexes. The Spider uses compound indexes for high-traffic queries, but the plans book will have low volume (one doc per planning run). Single-field indexes are sufficient."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Spec is explicit; low-volume book doesn't need compound index optimization."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S4 — 7 Tools
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D11
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should write tools validate that the plan exists before patching?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — get the plan first, throw if null, then patch"
      b: "No — let stacks.patch throw its own error if the doc doesn't exist"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Stacks Book.patch() already throws if the document doesn't exist ('Throws if the document does not exist' per types.ts). Adding a get-then-patch adds a redundant round-trip. The error message from Stacks is clear enough."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Stacks.patch already throws on missing doc; no need for redundant get."
      stakes: low

  - id: D12
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should scope-write and decisions-write validate the structure of their array params beyond Zod?"
    options:
      a: "No — Zod schema validation is sufficient at the tool boundary"
      b: "Yes — add semantic validation (unique IDs, valid scope references in decisions, etc.)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author, implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The Clerk's tools rely on Zod for input validation without additional semantic checks. These tools are called by planning animas — the risk of malformed input is low, and semantic issues would be caught during decision-review. Adding validation increases complexity without clear value for MVP."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Zod suffices at the boundary; anima callers produce well-formed data. Semantic validation can be added later if needed."
      stakes: low

  - id: D13
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should write tools also update the PlanDoc status (e.g. inventory-write sets status to 'analyzing')?"
    options:
      a: "No — tools only write their artifact field + updatedAt; status transitions are engine concerns"
      b: "Yes — tools advance the status machine automatically"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author, implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec assigns status transitions to specific engines (plan-init sets 'reading', decision-review sets 'reviewing'/'writing'). Tools are pure data writers — mixing status machine logic into tools would create competing state managers. The engines own the lifecycle."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Status transitions belong to engines per the architecture spec; tools are pure data writers."
      stakes: high

  - id: D14
    scope: [S4]
    question: "What Zod schema shape should scope-write use for ScopeItem[]?"
    options:
      a: "z.array(z.object({ id: z.string(), description: z.string(), rationale: z.string(), included: z.boolean() }))"
      b: "z.array(z.any()) — trust the caller"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Every tool in the codebase validates its params via Zod. ScopeItem has a clear, stable schema. Type-safe validation catches errors at the tool boundary."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Codebase convention: all tool params are validated via Zod schemas."
      stakes: low

  - id: D15
    scope: [S4]
    question: "What Zod schema shape should decisions-write use for Decision[]?"
    options:
      a: "Full schema with all Decision fields: id, scope (string[]), question, context?, options (record), recommendation?, rationale?, selected?, patronOverride?"
      b: "Minimal required fields only: id, scope, question, options"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The analyst writes the full Decision shape including recommendation and rationale. Optional fields should be validated when present but not required. This matches the interface definition."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Full schema with optional fields matches the Decision interface exactly."
      stakes: low

  - id: D16
    scope: [S4]
    question: "What permission namespace should the tools use?"
    options:
      a: "astrolabe:read for read tools, astrolabe:write for write tools"
      b: "A single astrolabe:* permission for all tools"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec explicitly states 'astrolabe:read' and 'astrolabe:write' permission namespaces. This matches the Clerk pattern (clerk:read, clerk:write)."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec is explicit; matches Clerk's read/write permission split."
      stakes: low

  - id: D17
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should plan-show return links (like writ-show returns writ + links)?"
    options:
      a: "No — return the PlanDoc only; plans don't have links"
      b: "Yes — include the associated writ info"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "PlanDocs don't have a link concept. The planId IS the writ ID, so the caller can look up the writ separately via writ-show. Adding writ data would couple the tool to the Clerk API unnecessarily."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Plans have no links; planId == writId enables separate writ lookup."
      stakes: low

  - id: D18
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should plan-list support orderBy customization?"
    options:
      a: "No — always order by createdAt desc (matching writ-list convention)"
      b: "Yes — add an optional orderBy param"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "writ-list hardcodes orderBy: ['createdAt', 'desc']. plan-list is a simpler query with low volume. No use case for alternative orderings."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Matches writ-list convention; no use case for alternative orderings."
      stakes: low

  - id: D19
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should plan-list include an offset param for pagination?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — include optional offset, matching writ-list"
      b: "No — limit only is sufficient for MVP"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "writ-list includes offset for pagination. Consistent API surface."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Matches writ-list convention for consistent API."
      stakes: low

  - id: D20
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should tools have instructions text for anima context?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — brief instructions string on each tool (matching writ-show, writ-list patterns)"
      b: "No — description only for MVP"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "Clerk tools include instructions strings that guide animas on when/how to use the tool. These are injected into the system prompt by the Loom. Planning animas will benefit from clear tool instructions."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Codebase convention for tools used by animas; aids planning session quality."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S5 — brief writ type
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D21
    scope: [S5]
    question: "What description should the brief writ type carry?"
    options:
      a: "'A patron brief triggering the planning pipeline'"
      b: "No description (name only)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [operator]
      confidence: high
      context: "WritTypeEntry supports an optional description. The writ-types tool surfaces it. A description helps patrons understand what the type does."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Low cost, meaningful for patron comprehension via writ-types tool."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S6 — astrolabe.sage role
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D22
    scope: [S6]
    question: "What permissions should the astrolabe.sage role grant?"
    options:
      a: "['astrolabe:read', 'astrolabe:write', 'clerk:read', 'clerk:write'] — read/write plans + read writs + post final mandate"
      b: "['astrolabe:read', 'astrolabe:write', 'clerk:read', 'clerk:write', 'stacks:read'] — also grant stacks read for book introspection"
      c: "['astrolabe:read', 'astrolabe:write'] — minimal; spec-writer posts writs via a separate mechanism"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: medium
      context: >
        The sage role is shared across reader, analyst, and spec-writer stages. The reader/analyst need astrolabe:read + astrolabe:write to call plan-show and the write tools. The spec-writer needs clerk:write to post the generated mandate writ, and clerk:read for writ-show/writ-link. The Loom validates that permissions reference plugins in the apparatus's requires — clerk is in requires, so clerk:read/write are valid. stacks:read is not needed because the anima doesn't call stacks tools directly.
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Covers all tool permissions needed across all three planning stages; clerk:write needed for final mandate posting."
      stakes: high

  - id: D23
    scope: [S6]
    question: "Should the sage role carry inline instructions or an instructionsFile?"
    options:
      a: "Inline instructions — short text describing the sage's purpose"
      b: "instructionsFile — point to a markdown file in the package"
      c: "Neither — no instructions for MVP"
    selected: c
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The sage role's behavior is differentiated by the per-engine prompt, not by the role instructions. Role instructions go into the system prompt for ALL stages. For MVP, the per-engine prompt (delivered via the rig template givens) is sufficient. Full role instructions can be added when the planning pipeline's prompt engineering is refined in a later commission."
      observable: true
      recommendation: c
      rationale: "Per-engine prompts differentiate stages; role instructions can be added later when prompt engineering is refined."
      stakes: low

  - id: D24
    scope: [S6]
    question: "Should the sage role be strict (exclude permissionless tools)?"
    options:
      a: "No — default (false): permissionless tools are included"
      b: "Yes — strict: only explicitly granted tools are available"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "No existing kit role in the codebase sets strict: true. Planning animas benefit from broad tool access (e.g. permissionless introspection tools). Strict mode would reduce capability unnecessarily."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "No precedent for strict mode in kit roles; broad tool access benefits planning animas."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S7 — Clockwork engines
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D25
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should engine design IDs be namespaced?"
    options:
      a: "'astrolabe.plan-init', 'astrolabe.inventory-check', 'astrolabe.decision-review' — dotted namespace"
      b: "'plan-init', 'inventory-check', 'decision-review' — bare names"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "Engine IDs must be globally unique in the Fabricator registry. Bare names risk collision with future engines from other kits. The architecture spec uses the dotted form ('astrolabe.plan-init'). The Fabricator stores by value.id, not by the supportKit engines key."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec uses dotted form; prevents global ID collision in Fabricator registry."
      stakes: high

  - id: D26
    scope: [S7]
    question: "Should plan-init validate that the writ has a codex field?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — throw if writ.codex is undefined (planning requires a codex)"
      b: "No — allow codex-less plans; store codex as empty string or undefined"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The PlanDoc.codex field is non-optional (string, not string?). Planning always targets a codex — the draft engine downstream will also fail without one. Failing early in plan-init with a clear error is better than failing later in draft with a confusing error."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "PlanDoc.codex is required; fail early with clear error rather than late with confusing one."
      stakes: low

  - id: D27
    scope: [S7]
    question: "Should plan-init check for an existing plan with the same ID before creating?"
    options:
      a: "No — use stacks.book.put() which upserts; a duplicate brief writ ID means the plan is reset"
      b: "Yes — throw if a plan with this writ ID already exists"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "Each brief writ should produce exactly one planning run. If a plan already exists for this writ ID, something is wrong (duplicate rig spawn, stale data). Throwing early surfaces the issue. However, the Spider already prevents duplicate rigs for the same writ, so this shouldn't happen in practice."
      observable: true
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Defensive — surfaces duplicate planning runs rather than silently resetting."
      stakes: low

  - id: D28
    scope: [S7]
    question: "What should inventory-check validate beyond inventory existence?"
    options:
      a: "Only check plan.inventory !== undefined (non-null/non-undefined)"
      b: "Also check that inventory is a non-empty string"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "An empty string inventory is functionally absent. The checkpoint exists to ensure the reader actually produced content. A non-empty check is a marginally better validation at zero cost."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Empty string is functionally absent; non-empty check is cheap and more meaningful."
      stakes: low

  - id: D29
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should decision-review detect first-run vs re-run?"
    options:
      a: "Check context.priorBlock presence — present means re-run after block cleared"
      b: "Check PlanDoc.status — 'analyzing' means first run, 'reviewing' means re-run"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The EngineRunContext.priorBlock field exists precisely for this purpose. It is set by the Spider when an engine is restarted after its block clears. The architecture spec describes the two-pass behavior using priorBlock. Checking PlanDoc status would work but couples the engine to status machine details unnecessarily."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "priorBlock exists for this purpose; architecture spec describes this pattern explicitly."
      stakes: low

  - id: D30
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should decision-review compose the details field for each ChoiceQuestionSpec?"
    options:
      a: "Concatenate context and rationale with a separator: context + '\\n\\nRecommendation rationale: ' + rationale"
      b: "Use context only; omit rationale from details"
      c: "Use rationale only; omit context from details"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec's mapping table shows 'context + rationale → questions[id].details'. Both pieces are useful for the patron: context explains the situation, rationale explains why the analyst recommends a particular option. Concatenation with a clear separator is natural."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec maps both fields to details; patron benefits from seeing both context and recommendation rationale."
      stakes: low

  - id: D31
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should decision-review handle a plan with no decisions (empty array or undefined)?"
    options:
      a: "Complete immediately with empty decisionSummary — no questions to ask"
      b: "Throw an error — analyst should always produce at least one decision"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "A plan with no decisions is valid — the brief might be simple enough that no choices are needed. Creating an InputRequestDoc with zero questions and then blocking would be pointless. Completing immediately allows the spec-writer to proceed."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "A plan with no decisions is valid; blocking for zero questions is pointless."
      stakes: low

  - id: D32
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should decision-review build the decisionSummary yield string?"
    options:
      a: "Human-readable markdown listing each decision with question, selected option, and patronOverride if present"
      b: "JSON string of the reconciled decisions array"
      c: "One-line summary: 'N decisions resolved, M patron overrides'"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec says decisionSummary is 'a human-readable string summarizing all decisions and the patron's selections, for injection into the spec-writer's prompt'. Markdown is the natural format for AI consumption. JSON would work but is harder to read in a prompt. A one-liner loses essential detail."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec says 'human-readable string'; markdown is natural for AI prompt consumption."
      stakes: low

  - id: D33
    scope: [S7]
    question: "How should decision-review compose the InputRequestDoc.message field?"
    options:
      a: "Include brief title, codex, and a summary of included scope items"
      b: "Include only the brief title"
      c: "Omit message entirely (field is optional)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec says 'The InputRequestDoc.message field carries a summary of the brief and scope context so the patron has orientation when reviewing decisions outside the planning conversation.' Title + codex + scope items gives the patron enough context."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec prescribes brief + scope context for patron orientation."
      stakes: low

  - id: D34
    scope: [S7]
    question: "Should decision-review update PlanDoc.status on first run and re-run?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — first run sets 'reviewing', re-run sets 'writing' (per architecture spec)"
      b: "No — leave status transitions to a separate mechanism"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec describes the status machine: decision-review transitions from analyzing→reviewing on first run, reviewing→writing on re-run. The engine is the natural owner of these transitions."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec prescribes these transitions; engine is the natural owner."
      stakes: low

  - id: D35
    scope: [S7]
    question: "Should decision-review reconcile scope item inclusion changes from the InputRequestDoc?"
    options:
      a: "No — MVP only reconciles decision answers (selected/patronOverride); scope changes are future"
      b: "Yes — include scope items as boolean questions in the InputRequestDoc"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The architecture spec's mapping table shows only Decision → ChoiceQuestionSpec mapping. ScopeItem inclusion is set by the analyst and confirmed by the patron, but the spec doesn't describe a mechanism for scope changes via InputRequestDoc. Adding scope as boolean questions is a reasonable future enhancement but not prescribed by the spec for MVP."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec only maps decisions to questions; scope inclusion changes are not prescribed for MVP."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S8 — Rig template
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D36
    scope: [S8]
    question: "How should the planId be delivered to anima-session engines, given that inline string interpolation is not yet available in the Spider?"
    options:
      a: "plan-init yields composed prompt strings (readerPrompt, analystPrompt, writerPrompt) with planId baked in; template uses $yields.plan-init.<stage>Prompt as the prompt given"
      b: "Prompts instruct the anima to call plan-list to discover the active plan"
      c: "Static prompts with no planId; anima discovers it from the writ context"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: high
      context: >
        The architecture doc lists 'givens inline string interpolation' as a prerequisite that has NOT yet landed. The Spider currently only resolves whole-value yield references ($yields.engineId.prop as the entire string value). The anima-session engine only passes role, prompt, cwd, conversationId, and writ to the Animator — extra givens like planId are ignored.

        Option (a) is the cleanest workaround: plan-init composes full prompt strings with the planId embedded, yielding them alongside planId. The template uses $yields.plan-init.readerPrompt etc. as whole-value yield refs for the prompt given. This is architecturally clean (one engine owns prompt composition) and works within current Spider capabilities.

        Option (b) is fragile — multiple concurrent plans could exist.
        Option (c) doesn't work — anima-session doesn't surface givens.writ to the AI.
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Only approach that reliably delivers planId to animas without inline string interpolation."
      stakes: high

  - id: D37
    scope: [S8]
    question: "What content should the reader, analyst, and spec-writer prompts contain for MVP?"
    options:
      a: "Minimal functional prompts — instruct each stage to call plan-show, perform its task, and write results via the appropriate tool"
      b: "Detailed multi-paragraph prompts with full pipeline instructions"
      c: "Placeholder strings — non-empty to pass anima-session validation, refined in a later commission"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The prompts must be non-empty (anima-session validates this). Placeholders would make the pipeline non-functional. Detailed prompts are premature for MVP — prompt engineering is iterative. Minimal functional prompts make the pipeline actually work end-to-end while leaving room for refinement."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Makes the pipeline functional end-to-end; detailed prompt engineering is iterative and belongs in a later commission."
      stakes: high

  - id: D38
    scope: [S8]
    question: "What should the rig template name be?"
    options:
      a: "'planning' — registered as 'astrolabe.planning'"
      b: "'astrolabe-planning' — more explicit"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "Kit template names are unqualified in the contribution; the Spider qualifies them as pluginId.templateName. So 'planning' becomes 'astrolabe.planning'. The 'astrolabe-' prefix would be redundant (resulting in 'astrolabe.astrolabe-planning')."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Spider auto-qualifies with pluginId; 'planning' yields clean 'astrolabe.planning' name."
      stakes: low

  - id: D39
    scope: [S8]
    question: "Should the rig template declare a resolutionEngine?"
    options:
      a: "No — omit; Spider falls back to seal engine or last completed engine"
      b: "Yes — set resolutionEngine to 'spec-writer' since that's where the meaningful output is"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [operator]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The seal engine yields { abandoned: true } which is not a meaningful resolution summary. The spec-writer's yields (sessionId, conversationId, output) contain the actual result. Setting resolutionEngine makes the rig's completion summary meaningful for operators."
      observable: true
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "spec-writer produces meaningful output; seal yields { abandoned: true } which is uninformative."
      stakes: low

  - id: D40
    scope: [S8]
    question: "How should the spec-writer receive the decisionSummary?"
    options:
      a: "As a separate given: decisionSummary: '$yields.decision-review.decisionSummary' — anima-session ignores extra givens, but the prompt (composed by plan-init) can reference plan-show instead"
      b: "Bake it into the prompt — plan-init can't know decisionSummary (it runs before decisions exist); the prompt just instructs the anima to call plan-show"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The plan-init engine runs at step 1 — long before decisions exist. It can't compose a spec-writer prompt that includes the decisionSummary. The spec-writer's prompt should instruct the anima to call plan-show to read the full plan state including reconciled decisions. The decision-review engine also updates the PlanDoc with reconciled decisions, so plan-show returns everything the spec-writer needs."
      observable: true
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "plan-init runs before decisions exist; spec-writer prompt instructs anima to call plan-show for current state."
      stakes: low

  - id: D41
    scope: [S7, S8]
    question: "Should the analyst prompt differ from the reader prompt in how conversation context is used?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — analyst prompt states it is continuing the reader's conversation and should build on that context"
      b: "No — treat all prompts identically"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec says 'resumes reader's conversation' for the analyst and 'resumes the analyst's conversation' for the spec-writer. The prompts should acknowledge the conversation continuation so the anima understands it has prior context to build on."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec describes conversation chaining; prompts should acknowledge continuation."
      stakes: low

  - id: D42
    scope: [S8]
    question: "What writ given should be passed to anima-session engines in the template?"
    options:
      a: "writ: '$writ' — passed to all anima-session engines (reader, analyst, spec-writer)"
      b: "Omit writ from anima-session givens — only plan-init and draft need it"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "anima-session uses writ for GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL and metadata.writId. Passing the writ to all three sessions ensures consistent metadata tracking and git identity across the pipeline."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Ensures consistent metadata tracking and git identity across all three sessions."
      stakes: low

  - id: D43
    scope: [S8]
    question: "Should plan-init yield prompt strings for all three stages, or should prompts be static in the template?"
    options:
      a: "plan-init yields readerPrompt, analystPrompt, writerPrompt — template uses yield refs"
      b: "Prompts are static strings in the template; planId is passed as a separate given"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "As established in D36, inline string interpolation is not available. The anima-session engine ignores extra givens (like planId). The only way to deliver planId to the anima is via the prompt string. plan-init is the engine that has the planId at yield time, so it must compose the prompts."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Directly follows from D36 — plan-init is the only engine that can compose prompts with planId."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S9 — Configuration
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D44
    scope: [S9]
    question: "Where should generatedWritType be resolved — in the apparatus start() or lazily in engine/tool handlers?"
    options:
      a: "Lazily at handler invocation time via guild().guildConfig().astrolabe?.generatedWritType ?? 'mandate'"
      b: "At start() — read and cache the value"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Loom reads its config at start() and caches it. The Clerk resolves config lazily in helpers. The generatedWritType is only used by the spec-writer (the anima that posts the final writ). Since the anima calls clerk tools directly (commission-post with a type param), the config value needs to be accessible to the anima's tool call or prompt — not cached in the apparatus. In practice, the prompt will instruct the anima to read the config or use a hardcoded default."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Config value is used lazily by the spec-writer anima's tool calls; caching at start() provides no benefit."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S10 — Tests
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D45
    scope: [S10]
    question: "What test file structure should be used?"
    options:
      a: "Single test file astrolabe.test.ts covering tools and engines — matching clerk.test.ts pattern"
      b: "Separate test files per concern: tools.test.ts, engines.test.ts"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "clerk.test.ts and fabricator.test.ts use a single file. The spider splits into spider.test.ts, input-request.test.ts, etc. because it's much larger. The astrolabe is moderate size — a single file with describe blocks per concern is appropriate."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Moderate-sized plugin; single file with describe blocks matches clerk/fabricator pattern."
      stakes: low

  - id: D46
    scope: [S10]
    question: "What should tests cover?"
    options:
      a: "Tools (CRUD operations, error cases), engines (plan-init, inventory-check, decision-review two-pass), supportKit shape validation"
      b: "Tools only — engines are integration-tested via spider"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Spider tests its own engines (draft, seal, review) in spider.test.ts. The astrolabe's clockwork engines have meaningful logic (decision-review's two-pass flow) that should be unit-tested independently. The in-memory Stacks pattern makes this straightforward."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "decision-review has complex two-pass logic that warrants direct testing; in-memory Stacks makes it easy."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Cross-cutting
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D47
    scope: [S1, S4, S7]
    question: "How should the apparatus access the plans book — via a module-level closure variable set in start(), or by calling guild().apparatus('stacks').book() in each handler?"
    options:
      a: "Module-level closure variable set in start() — matching Clerk pattern (let writs: Book<WritDoc>)"
      b: "Each handler calls guild().apparatus<StacksApi>('stacks').book<PlanDoc>('astrolabe', 'plans')"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Clerk (clerk.ts) uses closure variables: 'let writs: Book<WritDoc>; let links: Book<WritLinkDoc>;' assigned in start(). This avoids repeated apparatus lookup and book initialization on every tool call."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Matches Clerk pattern; avoids repeated guild().apparatus() lookups."
      stakes: low

  - id: D48
    scope: [S4, S7]
    question: "How should tools and engines access the plans book — through the same closure, or independently?"
    options:
      a: "Tools reference the closure variable; engines call guild().apparatus('stacks').book() independently"
      b: "Both use the closure variable from the apparatus factory"
      c: "Both call guild().apparatus('stacks').book() independently"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Clerk's tools reference the closure writs/links variables directly. The engines are defined inside the createAstrolabe() factory function and have access to the same closure scope. This keeps book access consistent."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Engines defined inside factory function share closure scope; consistent with how Clerk tools access books."
      stakes: low

  - id: D49
    scope: [S7]
    question: "Should clockwork engines be defined inline in the apparatus factory or in separate files under src/engines/?"
    options:
      a: "Separate files under src/engines/ — matching Spider's engine organization"
      b: "Inline in astrolabe.ts — simpler for a small plugin"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Spider organizes engines in src/engines/ with an index.ts barrel. The decision-review engine has significant logic (InputRequestDoc creation, answer reconciliation, summary building) that warrants its own file. Consistency with Spider conventions aids navigation."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "decision-review is complex enough to warrant its own file; matches Spider organization."
      stakes: low

  - id: D50
    scope: [S7, S4]
    question: "If engines are in separate files, how do they access the plans book closure variable?"
    options:
      a: "Engines receive a book accessor function from the factory; engine files export factory functions not bare EngineDesign objects"
      b: "Engines call guild().apparatus('stacks').book() directly — no closure dependency"
      c: "Engines are defined inline in astrolabe.ts despite separate file structure in inventory"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Spider's engines (draft, seal, review) all call guild().apparatus() at runtime inside their run() methods. They are defined as standalone EngineDesign objects in separate files with no factory pattern. This is the established convention. The closure optimization from D47/D48 applies to tools within the factory, but engines should follow Spider's pattern of direct guild() access."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Spider's engines call guild() directly at runtime; engines in separate files can't access the factory closure."
      stakes: low

content.content: File created successfully at: _planning/decisions.yaml (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

toolUseResult.content:
decisions:
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S1 — Package scaffolding
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D1
    scope: [S1]
    question: "What is the npm package name?"
    options:
      a: "@shardworks/astrolabe — per architecture spec (no -apparatus suffix)"
      b: "@shardworks/astrolabe-apparatus — matching existing apparatus packages in the repo"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [patron, author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec explicitly says 'Package: @shardworks/astrolabe · Plugin id: astrolabe'. Every other apparatus in the repo uses the -apparatus suffix, but the spec is the authoritative source."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "The architecture spec is explicit; follow it."
      stakes: high

  - id: D2
    scope: [S1]
    question: "What dependencies go in the apparatus requires array?"
    options:
      a: "['stacks', 'clerk', 'spider', 'loom', 'fabricator'] — per architecture spec"
      b: "['stacks', 'clerk'] — minimum needed at start(); others as recommends"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec explicitly lists all five. While loom and fabricator aren't called in start(), the spec is authoritative. Kit contribution consumption doesn't require requires (Wire phase handles it), but the spec captures a design intent about what must be installed."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Architecture spec is explicit and reflects a considered design intent."
      stakes: low

  - id: D3
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should @shardworks/loom-apparatus be added as an npm dependency for type-safe role definitions?"
    options:
      a: "No — use plain object for roles contribution; no loom import"
      b: "Yes — import KitRoleDefinition for type safety via satisfies"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The supportKit roles field is an open record — no type import is required. Adding loom as an npm dep couples the packages. Other kits in the repo contribute roles without importing loom types. However, type safety is valuable."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Follows the pattern of loose coupling via open records; avoids unnecessary cross-package npm dependency."
      stakes: low

  - id: D4
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should the apparatus declare a consumes field?"
    options:
      a: "Omit entirely — Astrolabe doesn't consume kit contributions from others"
      b: "Declare consumes: [] explicitly for clarity"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Clerk omits consumes; Spider declares it because it actually consumes. Omitting is the codebase convention for apparatuses that don't consume."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Codebase convention — only declare consumes when actually consuming."
      stakes: low

  - id: D5
    scope: [S1]
    question: "Should recommends include 'oculus' for dashboard support?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — recommends: ['oculus'], matching Clerk and Spider patterns"
      b: "No — omit recommends entirely for MVP"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "Clerk and Spider both recommend oculus because they contribute pages. The Astrolabe MVP doesn't contribute pages. Adding oculus as recommends would be forward-looking but premature."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "No pages contributed; recommends would be empty promise."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S2 — Types
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D6
    scope: [S2]
    question: "What is the exact shape of the PlanDoc type?"
    options:
      a: "Verbatim from architecture spec — all fields as documented"
      b: "Modify to add additional fields for MVP convenience"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec defines the PlanDoc precisely. The types are a contract for other plugins to import."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "The architecture spec defines the canonical type; implement verbatim."
      stakes: high

  - id: D7
    scope: [S2]
    question: "Should PlanDoc status include a 'reviewing' status for the decision-review phase?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — status union is 'reading' | 'analyzing' | 'reviewing' | 'writing' | 'completed' | 'failed' (as in spec)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec includes all six statuses. The decision-review engine transitions to 'reviewing' before blocking, and to 'writing' after patron answers are reconciled."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Spec defines the full status machine; no decision needed."
      stakes: high

  - id: D8
    scope: [S2]
    question: "What should AstrolabeApi expose?"
    options:
      a: "Empty object — tools access Stacks directly, no inter-plugin API needed for MVP"
      b: "Expose show/list/patch methods mirroring ClerkApi pattern"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The Clerk exposes a full API because other plugins (Spider, engines) call it programmatically. The Astrolabe's tools and engines all access Stacks directly — no external caller needs an AstrolabeApi in MVP. Adding methods now would duplicate what tools already do. The provides field still exists (empty object) for future extension."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "No external caller needs the API; engines use Stacks directly. provides field exists as empty object for future extension."
      stakes: low

  - id: D9
    scope: [S2]
    question: "Where should the PlanStatus type alias be defined?"
    options:
      a: "Inline union on the PlanDoc.status field"
      b: "Separate exported type alias PlanStatus"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The status values are referenced by plan-list filter params (z.enum needs the values), by engines for transitions, and potentially by external consumers. A named type makes this reusable."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Reusable across tools (plan-list filter) and engines (status transitions)."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S3 — Book declaration
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D10
    scope: [S3]
    question: "What indexes should the plans book declare?"
    options:
      a: "['status', 'codex', 'createdAt'] — per architecture spec"
      b: "Add compound indexes like ['status', 'codex'] for common queries"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec declares exactly these three indexes. The Spider uses compound indexes for high-traffic queries, but the plans book will have low volume (one doc per planning run). Single-field indexes are sufficient."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Spec is explicit; low-volume book doesn't need compound index optimization."
      stakes: low

  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # S4 — 7 Tools
  # ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  - id: D11
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should write tools validate that the plan exists before patching?"
    options:
      a: "Yes — get the plan first, throw if null, then patch"
      b: "No — let stacks.patch throw its own error if the doc doesn't exist"
    selected: b
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The Stacks Book.patch() already throws if the document doesn't exist ('Throws if the document does not exist' per types.ts). Adding a get-then-patch adds a redundant round-trip. The error message from Stacks is clear enough."
      observable: false
      recommendation: b
      rationale: "Stacks.patch already throws on missing doc; no need for redundant get."
      stakes: low

  - id: D12
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should scope-write and decisions-write validate the structure of their array params beyond Zod?"
    options:
      a: "No — Zod schema validation is sufficient at the tool boundary"
      b: "Yes — add semantic validation (unique IDs, valid scope references in decisions, etc.)"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author, implementer]
      confidence: medium
      context: "The Clerk's tools rely on Zod for input validation without additional semantic checks. These tools are called by planning animas — the risk of malformed input is low, and semantic issues would be caught during decision-review. Adding validation increases complexity without clear value for MVP."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Zod suffices at the boundary; anima callers produce well-formed data. Semantic validation can be added later if needed."
      stakes: low

  - id: D13
    scope: [S4]
    question: "Should write tools also update the PlanDoc status (e.g. inventory-write sets status to 'analyzing')?"
    options:
      a: "No — tools only write their artifact field + updatedAt; status transitions are engine concerns"
      b: "Yes — tools advance the status machine automatically"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [author, implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "The architecture spec assigns status transitions to specific engines (plan-init sets 'reading', decision-review sets 'reviewing'/'writing'). Tools are pure data writers — mixing status machine logic into tools would create competing state managers. The engines own the lifecycle."
      observable: true
      recommendation: a
      rationale: "Status transitions belong to engines per the architecture spec; tools are pure data writers."
      stakes: high

  - id: D14
    scope: [S4]
    question: "What Zod schema shape should scope-write use for ScopeItem[]?"
    options:
      a: "z.array(z.object({ id: z.string(), description: z.string(), rationale: z.string(), included: z.boolean() }))"
      b: "z.array(z.any()) — trust the caller"
    selected: a
    analysis:
      audience: [implementer]
      confidence: high
      context: "Every tool in the codebase validates its params via Zod. ScopeItem has a clear, stable schema. Type-safe validation catches errors at the tool boundary."
      observable: false
      recommendation: a
